shortDescription.text: Plugin signature verification disabled by default

fullDescription.text: The upload_pkg and upload_bundle methods default verify_signature to False, allowing installation of unsigned or tampered plugin packages. An attacker who compromises a plugin distribution channel can inject malicious code that will be executed without any integrity check.

shortDescription.text: Privilege escalation via backwards invocation into host app

fullDescription.text: PluginAppBackwardsInvocation.invoke_app allows any plugin to invoke any application within the same tenant using InvokeFrom.SERVICE_API privilege level. The only access control is tenant_id scoping, meaning a low-privilege plugin can invoke high-privilege apps (e.g., admin workflows) and exfiltrate data or trigger sensitive operations.

shortDescription.text: Unrestricted tool invocation from plugin backwards invocation

fullDescription.text: PluginToolBackwardsInvocation.invoke_tool allows plugins to invoke arbitrary tools (including builtin tools) within the host application with no scope restriction or allowlist. A malicious plugin can invoke any tool type (ToolProviderType) with arbitrary parameters, potentially achieving remote code execution through code-execution tools or SSRF through HTTP-based tools.

PluginEncrypter.invoke_encrypt exposes encrypt, decrypt, and cache-clear operations to plugins. A malicious plugin can decrypt arbitrary provider credentials for the tenant by supplying the appropriate namespace and identity, gaining access to API keys, secrets, and other sensitive configuration.

shortDescription.text: HTTP request deserialization vulnerable to request smuggling and header injection

fullDescription.text: The deserialize_request function in http_parser.py parses raw HTTP bytes into a Flask Request object without validating header names/values for injection characters (CR/LF), without enforcing Content-Length consistency with actual body length, and without restricting which headers can be set. An attacker can craft raw bytes that inject additional headers or smuggle requests.

shortDescription.text: Server-side request forgery via Host header in HTTP parser

fullDescription.text: In deserialize_request, the Host header from untrusted raw bytes is used directly to set SERVER_NAME and SERVER_PORT in the WSGI environ without validation. This can be exploited to manipulate server-side routing, redirect internal requests, or bypass host-based access controls.

shortDescription.text: Missing per-user authorization in backwards app invocation

fullDescription.text: PluginAppBackwardsInvocation.invoke_app creates or retrieves an EndUser if user_id is empty, and otherwise performs only a database lookup without verifying that the calling plugin is authorized to act on behalf of that user. A malicious plugin can impersonate any user within the tenant by supplying their user_id.

shortDescription.text: No size limit or content validation on plugin package upload

fullDescription.text: The upload_pkg method accepts arbitrary bytes as pkg with no size limit enforced at the application layer. Combined with disabled signature verification, this enables denial-of-service via large uploads and supply-chain attacks through malicious package content.

message.text: upload_pkg defaults verify_signature=False, allowing installation of unsigned plugin packages. An attacker compromising the plugin distribution channel can inject arbitrary code. The same issue exists in upload_bundle.

PluginAppBackwardsInvocation.invoke_app allows any plugin to invoke any app in the tenant with SERVICE_API privilege. No per-app permission check or plugin-scope restriction is enforced. A malicious plugin can invoke admin-level workflows, exfiltrate data from other apps, or trigger destructive operations.

PluginToolBackwardsInvocation.invoke_tool accepts arbitrary tool_type, provider, tool_name, and tool_parameters from plugin context with no allowlist or scope restriction. A malicious plugin can invoke any registered tool (including code execution, HTTP request, or file system tools) to achieve remote code execution or server-side request forgery.

PluginEncrypter.invoke_encrypt exposes decrypt operation to plugins, allowing a malicious plugin to decrypt arbitrary provider credentials by specifying the target namespace and identity. This leaks API keys and secrets for all configured providers in the tenant.

deserialize_request parses raw HTTP bytes without validating header names/values for CRLF injection, without verifying Content-Length matches actual body length, and without restricting sensitive headers. An attacker can inject arbitrary headers or craft malformed requests that are interpreted differently by upstream and downstream components.

The Host header from untrusted raw HTTP bytes is used to populate SERVER_NAME and SERVER_PORT in the WSGI environ without validation. This allows manipulation of server-side URL generation, routing decisions, and can bypass host-based access controls.

invoke_app allows plugins to specify an arbitrary user_id to impersonate any user in the tenant. When user_id is empty, it auto-creates an EndUser, and when provided, it performs a database lookup without verifying the calling plugin's authorization to act as that user. This enables cross-user data access and action impersonation.

upload_pkg accepts arbitrary bytes with no application-level size limit and signature verification disabled by default. This enables denial-of-service via oversized uploads and supply-chain attacks via malicious package content that passes no integrity check.
